claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period; Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

Let's start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child. I'm inquiring about my son Alex, who's 17 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess Alex's eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask:

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?**

(For example, this could include studying at school, college, A-levels, Scottish Highers, or approved training courses.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in any approved education or training at the moment. He finished school and decided not to continue with A-levels or college.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. I have a follow-up question:

**Has Alex started paid work, and if so, is he working 24 hours or more per week?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he has started working. He's got a full-time job at the supermarket, so yes, he's working more than 24 hours a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I have enough details now to assess Alex's eligibility for Child Benefit.

Based on what you've told me:
- Alex is 17 years old
- He is not in approved education or training
- He has started paid work for 24 hours or more per week

According to the Child Benefit rules, you stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Decision: You are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Unfortunately, you do not qualify for Child Benefit for Alex because he is working 24 hours or more per week and is not in approved education or training. The rules specifically state that Child Benefit stops when a child aged 16 or over starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week while not being in approved education or training.

If Alex's circumstances change in the future (for example, if he decides to return to education or training, or reduces his working hours), you may want to contact the Child Benefit Office to discuss your situation again.

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old. While Child Benefit can continue for children aged 16-19 if they remain in approved education or training, Alex has left school and is not continuing with A-levels, college, or any approved training. Additionally, Alex has started full-time paid employment at a supermarket, working more than 24 hours per week. According to the rules, Child Benefit stops when a child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, aged 17. Alex has left education without continuing to approved education or training, and has started full-time employment working more than 24 hours per week. Under the Child Benefit rules, payments stop when a child aged 16 or over works 24+ hours per week and is not in approved education or training. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old. While Child Benefit can continue for children aged 16-19 if they remain in approved education or training, Alex has left school and is not continuing with A-levels, college, or any approved training. Additionally, Alex has started full-time paid employment at a supermarket, working more than 24 hours per week. According to the rules, Child Benefit stops when a child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, aged 17. Alex has left education without continuing to approved education or training, and has started full-time employment working more than 24 hours per week. Under the Child Benefit rules, payments stop when a child aged 16 or over works 24+ hours per week and is not in approved education or training. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
